architectureSummary.overview: ServiceSelection is an Angular 12 Web Component (Angular Elements) embedded in a legacy HTML page. The component communicates with a versioned ASP.NET Core API — v1 endpoints for existing clients, v2 with richer responses for the Angular component.

questions.questionText: What is an Angular Web Component and how did you implement it in ServiceSelection?
questions.answer:
Angular Elements wraps an Angular component as a native Web Component (Custom Element). The resulting web component can be used in any HTML page with a simple script include — no Angular knowledge required by the host page.

// app.module.ts (Angular 12 — pre-standalone)
@NgModule({
  declarations: [ServiceSelectorComponent],
  imports: [BrowserModule, ReactiveFormsModule, HttpClientModule],
  entryComponents: [ServiceSelectorComponent]  // needed for elements
})
export class AppModule implements DoBootstrap {
  constructor(private injector: Injector) {}

  ngDoBootstrap() {
    // Convert Angular component to Custom Element
    const serviceSelector = createCustomElement(
      ServiceSelectorComponent,
      { injector: this.injector });

    // Register as a custom HTML element
    customElements.define('service-selector', serviceSelector);
  }
}

Host page usage (no Angular needed):

<!-- Legacy HTML page -->
<service-selector
  api-base-url="https://api.example.com"
  theme="dark"
  locale="en-IN">
</service-selector>

<!-- Include the Angular bundle -->
<script src="service-selector-element.js"></script>

Input properties become HTML attributes (api-base-url). Output events become DOM CustomEvents that the host page can listen to with addEventListener.
followUpQuestions: How do you pass data from the host page to the Angular element?
How do you handle Angular element updates when host page changes an attribute?
questions.importantForInterview: Web Component attributes are always strings. To pass complex objects, use JavaScript to set the element's property directly: element.config = { ... } (property binding, not attribute binding).

questions.questionText: How did you implement API versioning for the ServiceSelection project?
questions.answer:
ServiceSelection had two API consumers: legacy HTML pages (using v1) and the new Angular element (using v2). V2 returned richer responses without breaking existing v1 clients.

// Program.cs
builder.Services.AddApiVersioning(options =>
{
    options.DefaultApiVersion = new ApiVersion(1, 0);
    options.AssumeDefaultVersionWhenUnspecified = true;
    options.ReportApiVersions = true;
}).AddMvc();

// Controller
[ApiController]
[ApiVersion("1.0")]
[ApiVersion("2.0")]
[Route("api/v{version:apiVersion}/services")]
public class ServicesController : ControllerBase
{
    [HttpGet]
    [MapToApiVersion("1.0")]
    public async Task<ActionResult<IEnumerable<ServiceDto>>> GetV1()
    {
        // Legacy: flat list of services
        return Ok(await _service.GetFlatListAsync());
    }

    [HttpGet]
    [MapToApiVersion("2.0")]
    public async Task<ActionResult<ServiceGroupedDto>> GetV2()
    {
        // V2: grouped by category, with additional metadata
        return Ok(await _service.GetGroupedWithMetadataAsync());
    }
}

V1 URL: /api/v1/services (unchanged, existing clients keep working)
V2 URL: /api/v2/services (Angular element uses this)
followUpQuestions: How do you deprecate an API version?
What happens when a client hits a deprecated endpoint?
questions.importantForInterview: Mark deprecated versions with [ApiVersion("1.0", Deprecated = true)]. The api-deprecated-versions response header warns clients. Document the sunset date. Never remove a version without a long deprecation period — you don't control all clients.
